text: The patient that we're looking at today is a forty-year-old male who presented with bilateral inguinal hernias. The hernias were painless. He noted bilateral inguinal bulges after vigorous activity. He was active in sports. He had had no prior hernia surgery, and he had no gastrointestinal complaints.

We discussed different options, including surgery and alternatives. and decided that given his young age and his active lifestyle that a hernia repair was appropriate. I discussed the risks and benefits of open and laparoscopic repairs, and he was eager to have a laparoscopic inguinal hernia repair bilaterally.

The surgeon performs dissection in both the right and left iliac fossa, reducing the hernias and placing mesh in the preperitoneal space bilaterally. Two separate pieces of mesh are used and minimally tacked to the undersurface of the inguinal canals, primarily utilizing Cooper's ligament.

The patient is positioned supine on the operating table. The surgeon and assistant are on opposite sides, utilizing three laparoscopic ports: a 10mm structural balloon cannula below the umbilicus, and two 5mm cannulae in the mid-hypogastric and suprapubic regions. An incision is made beneath the umbilicus to the rectus fascia. The anterior rectus fascia is divided, and a balloon dissector is inserted beneath the right rectus muscle and posterior rectus fascia, advanced to the pubic symphysis, expanded, and replaced with a structural balloon cannula.

The surgeon uses a ten millimeter thirty degree laparoscope to visualize the preperitoneal space, which is insufflated to eleven millimeters of mercury. Blunt dissection is performed with the laparoscope to identify anatomical features, including the linea alba. The initial five millimeter cannula is placed in the suprapubic position, and the left rectus muscle is visible. The mid-hypogastric five-millimeter cannula is inserted into the preperitoneal space through the linea alba.

The laparoscope is positioned just to the right of the linea alba as dissection begins. The inferior epigastric artery and vein are visible on the right side of the screen. The surgeon carefully exposes the right-sided Cooper's ligament using gentle sweeping motions to avoid avulsing blood vessels. The iliopubic tract is also visible horizontally alongside Cooper's ligament.

The surgeon uses one grasper to retract the abdominal wall upward while another grasper pulls downward, functioning as blunt dissectors. A direct hernia is visible medial to the epigastric vessels, with preperitoneal fat protruding into the direct space. The graspers provide tactile feedback through tissue resistance during manipulation.

The surgeon identifies the epigastric vessels over the lateral tube and above the direct space, with Cooper's ligament visible below. The iliac vein is located as the lateral-most structure during dissection along Cooper's ligament, and the surgeon points to it, noting its fullness and respiratory variations.

The surgeon dissects between Cooper's ligament and the iliopubic tract, revealing the iliac vein clearly. The femoral space is visible, where a femoral hernia would typically be located, just medial to the iliac vein. A fatty-looking blob is observed, potentially indicating a small femoral hernia, and is carefully dissected.

The surgeon identifies an iliac lymph node just medial to the iliac vein, noting its resistance to manipulation, and decides to leave it in place. The dissection continues from medial to lateral, with preperitoneal fat being removed from the dissection area to facilitate mesh placement. The iliopubic tract is visible, and a small direct hernia is observed just above it. The inferior epigastric artery and vein are seen marking the boundary between the deep inguinal ring and the direct space.

The surgeon carefully dissects preperitoneal fat from the iliac fossa to prevent mesh crinkling during placement. The iliac vein is visible, and dissection proceeds laterally over the iliac artery. The surgeon uses a grasper to stabilize vascular structures while pulling away to avoid bleeding from small branches. The edge of the peritoneum is seen adherent to the spermatic cord, which is becoming more visible.

The surgeon identifies the vas deferens, a bright white structure of the spermatic cord, and carefully peels the adherent peritoneum downward. The peritoneal membrane is visible below, and the surgeon cautiously avoids grasping the spermatic cord while searching for associated blood vessels. Preperitoneal fat is peeled from the right iliac fossa, with attention to avoid injuring sensory nerves such as the iliohypogastric, ilioinguinal, and genitofemoral nerves.

The surgeon examines the peritoneum adherent to the spermatic cord, noting it does not pass through the deep inguinal ring, indicating it is not a true indirect inguinal hernia. The iliac vein and artery are visible, with the spermatic cord overlaying the iliac artery. The surgeon peels down preperitoneal fat to ensure unobstructed mesh placement. The femoral space is inspected, revealing a lymph node and the iliopubic tract. A small direct hernia is identified, though its size does not fully explain the patient's bilateral inguinal hernias. The dissection of the right iliac fossa is completed, and the surgeon proceeds to the left iliac fossa.

The surgeon identifies Cooper's ligament on the left side of the surgical field, using it as a landmark to locate the epigastric vessels and the iliac artery and vein. The bladder is visible beneath the fatty tissue, and care is taken to avoid puncturing it. The surgeon gently sweeps along Cooper's ligament, carefully pulling down preperitoneal fat to reveal vascular structures, including the epigastric vein and possibly the epigastric artery, while continuing the dissection laterally to locate the iliac vein.

The surgeon carefully dissects around the spermatic cord on the left side, visible along with the inferior epigastric vessels at the upper part of the screen. Using graspers, the surgeon encircles the cord to examine it closely, ensuring no hernia sac or excess preperitoneal fat remains attached.

The surgeon carefully dissects the peritoneum from the spermatic cord on the left side, revealing preperitoneal fat that was not initially apparent. The peritoneum is pulled down, exposing the cord structures, which appear normal. Upon examining the area near the deep inguinal ring, the surgeon identifies and gently extracts a sizable piece of preperitoneal fat from the inguinal canal, potentially explaining the patient's symptomatic hernia.

The surgeon returns to the right side, dissecting the spermatic cord near the deep inguinal ring. Preperitoneal fat is identified extending into the inguinal canal, causing bilateral bulges. The surgeon carefully applies traction to peel away the fat, revealing the iliac artery below and noting the presence of lymph nodes embedded in the somewhat bloody fat. The major piece of fat is removed from the deep inguinal ring, preparing the area for mesh coverage.

The surgeon examines the empty deep inguinal ring after reducing preperitoneal fat bilaterally. A six by four inch piece of medium weight polypropylene mesh is introduced into the preperitoneal space beneath the inguinal canal. The mesh is initially turned ninety degrees but is adjusted to assume its proper open configuration.

The surgeon maneuvers a six by four inch polypropylene mesh into the preperitoneal space beneath the inguinal canal, ensuring the long axis points deep into the pelvis. The mesh is oriented correctly and pulled into position, hanging loosely without folding, suspended by tacks placed into Cooper's ligament and the muscle above, adhering to the rule that all tacks must be above the iliopubic tract.

The surgeon uses absorbable polyglycolic acid tacks to secure the upper edge of the mesh, ensuring it covers the small direct hernia. The first tack is placed into Cooper's ligament, and the mesh is flattened to assess its coverage and stability. The surgeon carefully positions the mesh to overlap the defect, avoiding impingement on sensory nerves by tacking above the iliopubic tract.

The surgeon uses a tacker as a blunt dissector to maneuver the mesh into position over the hernia site. The mesh is freed from tissue adhesions by sweeping behind it, allowing it to be positioned correctly. Counter pressure is applied to the abdominal wall while placing tacks along the superior edge of the mesh to secure it. The mesh hangs without crimping, covering the deep ring and direct space on the right side. On the left side, the mesh is tacked along Cooper's ligament, covering the indirect preperitoneal fat hernia.
